bibliographic_data.title: NFIX regulates neural progenitor cell differentiation during hippocampal morphogenesis.
bibliographic_data.abstract: During nervous system formation, one of the most important developmental events to occur is the differentiation of neural progenitor cells into neurons and glia. The embryonic forebrain provides a cogent example of this, with neural progenitor cells within the proliferative ventricular zone executing a program of proliferation, then differentiation, to generate the postmitotic cells of the cortex and hippocampus (Sauvageot and Stiles 2002). The abnormal proliferation or differentiation of cortical neural progenitor cells during development can lead to severe functional consequences, such as lissencephaly and microcephaly, both of which can cause mental retardation (Manzini and Walsh 2011). As such, understanding the regulatory processes controlling whether neural progenitor cells either divide and self-renew or exit the cell cycle and differentiate is critical to our understanding of both normal and pathological cortical development. NFIX plays a central role in hippocampal morphogenesis, regulating the formation of neuronal and glial populations within this structure.

core_content.novelty_claim: NFIX is shown to play a central role in regulating neural progenitor cell differentiation during hippocampal development, specifically through the repression of SOX9 expression.
core_content.key_methodology: The study used NFIX knockout mice and demonstrated that the absence of NFIX leads to delayed neural progenitor cell differentiation and abnormal morphology of the dentate gyrus. The mechanism involves upregulation of SOX9 in the hippocampus of NFIX knockout mice, with NFIX repressing Sox9 promoter-driven transcription.
outcomes: NFIX delays neural progenitor cell differentiation within the embryonic hippocampus.
NFIX is essential for the formation of both neuronal and glial populations within the hippocampus.
SOX9 is a target for transcriptional repression by NFIX.
core_content.full_text_markdown: During nervous system development, neural progenitor cells differentiate into neurons and glia. The embryonic forebrain provides an example where these cells undergo proliferation followed by differentiation to form postmitotic cells in the cortex and hippocampus. Abnormalities in this process can lead to severe functional consequences such as lissencephaly and microcephaly, which cause mental retardation. Understanding how neural progenitor cells decide between division and differentiation is crucial for both normal and pathological development. This study demonstrates that NFIX plays a central role in regulating the formation of neuronal and glial populations within the hippocampus by delaying progenitor cell differentiation and repressing SOX9 expression.
